### `id`

integer

### `date_modified`

string (date-time)

The last moment when the item was modified. A value in year 1750 indicates the value is unknown

Lookups: `day`, `gt`, `gte`, `hour`, `lt`, `lte`, `minute`, `month`, `range`, `second`, `year`

### `date_created`

string (date-time)

The moment when the item was created.

Lookups: `day`, `gt`, `gte`, `hour`, `lt`, `lte`, `minute`, `month`, `range`, `second`, `year`

### `date_filed`

string (date)

The date the case was filed.

Lookups: `day`, `gt`, `gte`, `lt`, `lte`, `month`, `range`, `year`

### `date_terminated`

string (date)

The date the case was terminated.

Lookups: `day`, `gt`, `gte`, `lt`, `lte`, `month`, `range`, `year`

### `date_last_filing`

string (date)

The date the case was last updated in the docket, as shown in PACER's Docket History report or iquery page.

Lookups: `day`, `gt`, `gte`, `lt`, `lte`, `month`, `range`, `year`

### `docket_number`

string

The docket numbers of a case, can be consolidated and quite long. In some instances they are too long to be indexed by postgres and we store the full docket in the correction field on the Opinion Cluster.

### `docket_number_core`

string

For federal district court dockets, this is the most distilled docket number available. In this field, the docket number is stripped down to only the year and serial digits, eliminating the office at the beginning, letters in the middle, and the judge at the end. Thus, a docket number like 2:07-cv-34911-MJL becomes simply 0734911. This is the format that is provided by the IDB and is useful for de-duplication types of activities which otherwise get messy. We use a char field here to preserve leading zeros.

Lookups: `startswith`

### `nature_of_suit`

string

The nature of suit code from PACER.

Lookups: `contains`, `endswith`, `icontains`, `iendswith`, `iexact`, `istartswith`, `startswith`

### `pacer_case_id`

string

The case ID provided by PACER.
